text:
What's the value of a GPT wrapper? 

I feel like I've had this discussion a few times now, and people consistently underestimate it. Over the past six months, it’s become especially clear that people are willing to pay for GPT wrappers—but why?

I think it comes down to the “fear of the blank page,” or the terror of starting from scratch. Prompting itself is a skill. Even when the only added value is a prompt and a user interface over an LLM, that can already be intensely valuable. Most people don’t fully grasp the state of the art, they don’t know the model’s capabilities, and they’re often unaware that a general-purpose AI tool can handle so many tasks.

What also surprises me—personally—is how often I discover new tricks or hacks to get the output I want. There’s a meta-programming aspect here: you must not only understand what you’re trying to do, but also iterate on your prompt to guide the model’s output properly. Essentially, you have two tasks: (1) accomplishing your original goal, and (2) figuring out how to get the LLM to achieve that goal.

I personally find the prompt-engineering “game” engaging. But many people would rather skip the prompt-creation process entirely and just get the result. This is why, for example, vibecoding is so appealing: it removes the friction of careful prompting because you just dump in the errors, and the model solves them. No additional prompting required.

Returning to the main point: GPT wrappers are valuable because they solve the problem of figuring out how to prompt the model in order to get the right output. They also provide affordances on the LLM. For instance, in tools like Cursor or Claude, the UI allows you to select a line and have the model change that line specifically.

So, these wrappers and interfaces expand the LLM’s range of motion for the average user, making them feel comfortable and confident. General-purpose tools are inherently tough to master, so having a layer that guides the user and removes guesswork is why GPT wrappers are a goated business.

text:
SEO for When You Don’t Really Care About SEO

1. The Importance of Backlinks

What they are: A backlink is when another website links to yours. Google uses backlinks to help determine your site’s authority and ranking.
Quality over quantity: Links from reputable sites (e.g., CNN, BBC) provide significant boosts; links from spammy sites can hurt you.
Dofollow vs. Nofollow: A dofollow link transfers ranking “credit,” whereas nofollow links do not. Most natural links in articles are dofollow.
Simply be aware that backlinks are important. If someone likes your site, see if they’ll link to it. If you get press coverage or do interviews, make sure they include a dofollow link. You don’t have to buy or heavily engineer backlinks if you’re not trying to “game” the system.

2. Structuring Your Content

Use relevant keywords: If you want to rank for a particular topic, include those keywords in the title and the body of your post. For example, if you’re writing about “Anki,” make sure “Anki” appears in the title.
Match user searches: Think about what search terms people actually use. Incorporating those exact phrases into your content can help Google match you with relevant searches.
Website optimization: Make sure your site isn’t slow or error-prone. A fast, stable site typically ranks higher. Tools such as Lighthouse or Google’s PageSpeed Insights can help you spot performance issues.

3. Using Ahrefs (or Similar Tools)

What it does: Ahrefs provides insights on backlinks, site authority, and the keywords that drive traffic to your site.
Why it’s useful: Even if you’re not deeply invested in SEO, it helps to know who’s linking to you and which topics bring in search traffic.
Practical setup: It’s free to monitor your own site. Install it, check basic reports occasionally, and note any big spikes or dips in your search traffic.

4. Meta Tags & Basic Technical SEO

Meta tags: Properly formatted titles, meta descriptions, and other tags help Google understand what your content is about.
Length considerations: Titles and meta descriptions should typically adhere to Google’s preferred length (around 50–60 characters for titles, 150–160 for meta descriptions).
WordPress plugins: If you use WordPress, plugins like Yoast SEO make it easy to follow best practices (e.g., alerting you when your title is too long or if you haven’t used your keyword enough).

5. Maintaining Consistent URLs

Avoid broken links: If Google sees many broken links, it can harm your ranking.
Use redirects: If you move or rename articles, set up a 301 redirect so visitors and Google can follow the new URL without penalty.

Final Thoughts

Even if you don’t care about advanced SEO techniques, following these basics helps ensure you’re not sabotaging your website’s visibility. Most steps—like keeping your site fast, organizing your content, and being mindful of backlinks—are also just good web practices. By spending a few hours on setup and then following simple guidelines, you’ll capture the majority of SEO benefits without the hassle of deep optimization.

text:
10/10 

I don't think you can find a better book about oysters than this one. I really appreciated that it didn't dive into excessive, nerdy details about every type of oyster — instead, it offered a brief, clear explanation. 

The recipes are excellent, I tested a few, and they turned out great. The illustrations and colors are wonderful, and the book itself is a beautiful ornament. It truly inspires a joy of oysters in me.

text:
10/10 if you haven't been exposed to these ideas before
Otherwise, 8/10

This book was rather confusing to me. While I completely understood its central message and found its diagnosis both worthwhile and accurate, I felt that it struggled to provide effective solutions. Many of the concepts were already familiar, circulating in TPOT, and it was valuable to have them all compiled and clearly explained in one place. 

I found it challenging, however, to implement the ideas. In particular, Self 1 feels more like an emotional takeover, and Gallwey's techniques did not work well for me. 

It's hard to critique the book further because its influence has permeated my environment, a bit of a Seinfeld effect, if you will. 

I wonder if anyone has ever managed to get their "Self 1" functioning at a pro athlete level?

text:
7/10

I'm a sucker for anything related to Arnold Schwarzenegger, so I enjoyed this book. However, it felt weaker than his previous autobiography, Total Recall. 

It’s essentially a blend of autobiography and self-help. Arnold provides some added perspective on his life, what worked for him and what didn’t — and it serves as decent inspiration. It’s a good, quick read, perfect for an airplane.

text:
9/10

I'm a sucker for masks. I wish the book had a more documentary style, but I have to admit that the images are quite striking and well-edited. As a coffee table book, it's meant to be visually appealing rather than strictly documentary, though I do feel there's room for more content. 

The images capture the experience of encountering masks in person, which is really appealing. Plus, the book has a wonderful global focus, featuring pieces from every inhabited continent, and it leaves me wanting more.

text:
the most important overlooked aspect of language learning is the social part. llm's don't (really) help you with this. at some point you'll have to sound dumb in front of a real person. it's better to front load this

a more general point here is that for your median person — better off with italki. there's also a social coercion happening when you schedule a lesson. it's super easy to blow off an LLM available at any time, much harder to blow off a real lesson with a real person that costs real money

having said that though, in terms of actual language learning, i think it'd be super valuable if adv voice mode was 30% better. you could get tailored input for your skill level, content that's interesting to you, and get corrections on your output

i don't think you could ever get rid of a tutor unless LLMs get an OOM better, but you could totally do 3x as many lessons with an LLM as with the tutor if they get 20% better. tone, sounding natural, getting adjusted to the culture behind the language, addressing unknown unknowns are all things that current LLMs would not be great at, hence the need for a tutor

good questions, just my 2c

text:
I think you can hold both pro-tech and anti-tech mindsets at the same time — our social technology, the norms, rules and rituals that govern how we use tech, is  underdeveloped.

On one hand, technology is unambiguously good. Lives are saved, hours of work are wiped away, and the capabilities delivered to the average person are incredible. It’s impossible imo to argue that it’s “bad.” To give all that up would require a totally different set of axioms about what life is about.

On the other hand, technology has reshaped our world in ways that are detrimental to most, while boosting the leverage of a few ultra-capable people. Many technologies eventually embed social rules—think of “no phones at dinner,” which used to be a strict faux pas but has now been eroded by the sheer addictiveness of smartphones. The problem isn’t the device itself, but our lack of developing and enforcing the social norms around it.

People tend to resign themselves to necessary evils in order to reap the benefits of tech, but we just haven’t figured out the correct social tech for smartphones and social media. Imagine if it were universally frowned upon to use your phone in restaurants, or if public settings had enforced tech-free periods (see Iberian blackout).

Still, I think we’re starting to see progress: locking phones in classrooms is now widespread! A great example of a social norm spreading to schools across the US!

text:
Mamdani says "Paris has even stronger unions and yet the trains cost much less. It isn't the work rules or the labor rules in general that drive cost"

Frankly, this is just false — I don't think I can do better than this NY Times report on subway costs, so I'm just going to give some choice screenshots and a link

It even has a comparison to Paris!

For what it's worth unions aren't the only reason for increased costs — and maybe we as a society are okay with paying those costs! But it's disingenuous to pretend this isn't a factor

https://t.co/yKJ8JKn89F

text:
what i learned not looking at screens for 3 weeks

it was extremely pleasant. i had some minor like withdrawal symptoms for the first 6 hours where I kept wanting to check my phone, but after that I was relieved that I no longer was drawn into screen staring

i started off by having my gf check all my messages for me, which was wonderful <3. we ended up on settling for a 20 minute session where I'd bulk reply to all important messages, since dictating was becoming onerous. 

i also needed to carry the phone around sometimes when meeting up with people who didn't have my dumb phone's number. i did a pretty decent job of not touching it unless i actually needed to.

i wish i could say i got some crazy superpowers, but it was exactly what you'd expect. i felt like i had more time. my day didn't slip by like it used to. i got a hell of a lot more reading done, to the tune of one book every 3 days  — reading pretty much replaced my screen time 1:1.

i stopped looking things up on google, instead i wrote them into a notebook to then check up on later. frankly, it helped me stay a bit more connected to the world around me by avoiding the wikipedia rabbit holes. the optimal amount of rabbitholing is definitely nonzero, but i think i was on the far side of the healthy amount.

i had a lot more energy to do things than usual. the big confounder for this was that i wasn't working, but i feel comfortable saying that at least part was staying off the screens. i get couch locked by devices super easily, so getting up to do chores was much easier when i was reading a book than scrolling twitter.

i need to shift my life away from excessive internet content — this was super pleasant and i'd like to continue. the downside, of course, is that i missed out on lovely blogs like patio11s bits about money and works in progress magazine. it's easy enough to say that i should just stop browsing the internet outside of proven good content, but then that breaks the discovery mechanism. i'd want to do more exploit than explore, but if the exploring is essential yet mostly a waste of time, what the hell do i do?

i recommend trying it, if nothing else it was a very nice way to spend 3 weeks

text:
unemployment reviews lightning round 

wild food foraging with wildman steve brill - pretty fun, but it really was just a lot of greens. the value i got out of it was recognizing more plants. the flavors were ...ok, but not great

hiking trail repair - tons of fun, did it twice, will definitely do again. it's a volunteer outfit that specializes in building rock stairs. sometimes you just wanna do some manual labor. the stairs last 50 - 100 years so it's nice that you can hike on what you've built for the rest of your life

picnic in central park - goated, leisure-maxxing, aristocrat coded. need i say more?

cafe sabarsky - 10/10 on vibes, 5/10 on food. worth going regardless. it's funny that even though it looks european, the people in the cafe have a little bit too much cortisol and it ruins the illusion.

archery - pretty fun, but i didn't feel a strong attraction to continue.

rockefeller tour - i didn't realize you could go inside the 30 Rockefeller Plaza lobby! amazing murals. the tour was great, it was wonderful to get the full story about my favorite building(s) in NYC. lots of fun little tidbits about the artwork — which i learned was added in spite of Rockefeller Jr's opposition

pistol shooting - great fun! will definitely be going again. despite it being my first time i had pretty decent aim. i'm glad that i doubled up on hearing protection since it was an indoor range.

transit museum - it was worth visiting once, but tbh felt like it was designed by artists, not engineers. this isn't necessarily bad, but i wished there was more about the construction, operation, and engineering of the subway system. still worth going though, especially if you're a train nerd.

walk across manhattan, top to bottom - 10/10 experience. it's around 30k steps, 13 miles — i'd say not too effortful. i really enjoyed seeing inwood since i'd never been there before. it was also quite funny realizing how much of manhattan is above 96th st! 

horseback riding - fucking terrifying. i was scared the entire time, nobody warned me how massive horses are. imagine — you're being jostled around in the saddle by the horse's gait, excruciatingly aware of how much more powerful the big guy under you is and that you're only there because he's allowing you to sit on him. shockingly enough the horse actually listened to me ~80% of the time, so we successfully made it through some cones. if i go do riding again, i'll probably need to make it my life for around 6 months, it really doesn't seem like a casual hobby you can pop into.

sailing - it was fine? but frankly a bit boring for me. i felt hot, sweaty, and slightly dehydrated. i much prefer kayaking where i have to exert more effort — i feel more engaged. i just think i'm not a water type pokemon.

museum of city of ny - it suffered from the same issue that the transit museum did — felt designed by artists. also, what's with this trend of museums being super empty inside? give me museum maximalism, i want my head to spin because of the sheer number of exhibited artifacts. i also felt that it was unclear on the target audience, a tourist would likely be bored, and a new yorker would not be surprised by anything on display. i felt like the museum could've dialed up the depth and amount of content by quite a lot.

deer scouting - tons of fun, i'm happy that i finally got off trail and gain a better understanding of nature. deer scouting is where you try to track down the deer before going hunting, so it involves looking at maps, visiting likely spots, and finding deer sign like poop or tree rubs. it's a lot slower than hiking and you need to really think about where you want to go next, putting yourself in the mind of a deer.

10/10 would recommend being unemployed for a short bit

text:
screen free unemployment book reviews

Death Is Our Business by Lechner - an interesting book, really more of a current events journalism piece — filled in a lot of knowledge gaps i had about Wagner and Russia's African interests. 9/10, wish it had yet more detail (which i'm not certain is possible)

Why Nations Fail - tbh i was very underwhelmed. i wish i was qualified to write a real takedown of this, but basically my qualms are that the institutions argument is almost tautological. what generates these good institutions? how do they stay around? a useful book to stay current in the larger conversation, but i found it  simplistic? 6/10

Congo: The Epic History of a People by Reybrouck - a wonderful, wonderful book. basically a history of the congo, with a focus on the congolese perspective, not the western one. due to a paucity of congolese sources, he's forced to rely on oral history a lot, but i think this gives the book a personal and grounded character. many eye opening bits in the book, especially surrounding independence. if you're curious about the congo or about african governance in general, this is a key read in my opinion. 10/10

The State of Africa by Meredith - a good summary book that sort of gets you up to speed on what's happened in post colonial africa. it does a solid job of providing an overview while keeping you engaged by flitting between various countries. it zooms into the personal with some frequency, which also helps pacing and retaining my engagement. 8/10

The Dragon's Path - read this on patio11's rec, but frankly found this one a bit weak. not nearly enough banking tbh. i also found the plot thin. 5/10

everything past this point i didn't finish ->

Out There: The Wildest Stories from Outside Magazine - a few pretty fun stories, i enjoyed the one about mongolian horse racing. 7/10

Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain - got about halfway. seemed to be working? the upside down drawing really did have a good result! i'd like to continue if i have more time. the brain stuff seems a bit woo, but as a drawing handbook seems solid 8/10

WEIRDest People in the World - i got about halfway before realizing it was just going to be yet more evidence pointing to the same conclusion. i enjoyed this book because of the mountain of proof presented. one specific feature i really appreciated was how graphs were inserted directly into the text, so you can verify yourself that these isn't some ridiculous stats hacking behind the scenes. overall, this was far more compelling to me than Why Nations Fail, even if it was mono-causal. 9/10

Read bits and pieces of 
Democracy in America
Book of Mark, KJV

and continued on with Moby Dick (still goated), Count of Monte Cristo, and Theoderic the Great

Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain - got about halfway. seemed to be working? the upside down drawing really did have a good result! i'd like to continue if i have more time. the brain stuff seems a bit woo, but as a drawing handbook seems solid 8/10

WEIRDest People in the World - i got about halfway before realizing it was just going to be yet more evidence pointing to the same conclusion. i enjoyed this book because of the mountain of proof presented. one specific feature i really appreciated was how graphs were inserted directly into the text, so you can verify yourself that these isn't some ridiculous stats hacking behind the scenes. overall, this was far more compelling to me than Why Nations Fail, even if it was mono-causal. 9/10

Read bits and pieces of 
Democracy in America
Book of Mark, KJV

and continued on with Moby Dick (still goated), Count of Monte Cristo, and Theoderic the Great

the criticism that i have of self help books in general as a frequent consumer is that 

1. they're not that deep. which frankly, is kind of the point. usually there's one good idea drawn out into book form, as a proto-spaced repetition.

3. the value lies entirely in the reader's execution

text:
This was a 10/10 movie experience.

- got so many chills on the opening scenes of the desert
- it's incredible how the movie mixes frivolity and laughter with dead serious plot beats and gets away with it. eg when lawrence and his guide are going to prince faisal and lawrence falls off his camel
- the way the characters behave is just so good
-  the symbolism is pretty clear, without beating you over the head with it. eg lambs during turkish column assault
- didn't feel long, the pacing was great. i think it's different when you see it in theaters — you're waiting holding your breath when the figure appears on the horizon at the well
- lots of subtlety in the acting. my second watch and i noticed a hell of a lot i didn't see the first time

an amazing amazing film. tbh a totally different experience on the big screen

raw.full_text:
This was a 10/10 movie experience.

- got so many chills on the opening scenes of the desert
- it's incredible how the movie mixes frivolity and laughter with dead serious plot beats and gets away with it. eg when lawrence and his guide are going to prince faisal and lawrence falls off his camel
- the way the characters behave is just so good
-  the symbolism is pretty clear, without beating you over the head with it. eg lambs during turkish column assault
- didn't feel long, the pacing was great. i think it's different when you see it in theaters — you're waiting holding your breath when the figure appears on the horizon at the well
- lots of subtlety in the acting. my second watch and i noticed a hell of a lot i didn't see the first time

an amazing amazing film. tbh a totally different experience on the big screen

text:
see this is why zoning is a topic i care deeply about. my family wants to come visit me and they'd like to stay in a hotel. 

the hotels in new york cost like $400 a night and most are near times square, which is the shittiest part of the city

because of *various reasons* hotel construction is exceedingly difficult, mostly relating to permitting, but also generally because of zoning.

and this is my point — my life is being shaped by regulation. so is yours. and nobody notices this!

who has chosen to take their vacation somewhere else because the hotels in nyc were insanely priced? who has a bad opinion of nyc because they had to stay in times square?

take the zoning-pill, see how deep the rabbit hole goes.

text:
I've been enjoying "Medici".

It has a little bit of competence porn. Lots of fun tidbits about banking, woven into the story well.

The show is engaging. Some of the acting can be poor. It has this strange issue where indeterminate amounts of time happen between plot beats.

I also enjoy that the writers did not throw the atheism of our age onto this period. People in the show are actually earnestly religious.

8/10, recommend if you like history and banking

@patio11 you'll enjoy this one!

text:
i've set up an alfred workflow that prepends notes directly into a txt with a timestamp. this has been great since it's super easy on the write, but not great on read. sample usage below

"jot make sure to review the document by tomorrow"
where jot is the keyword to trigger the workflow

i'm debating how to improve this — i can manually write in a short all caps topic at the start of my note like

"jot X_PROJECT make sure to review the document by tomorrow"

or i could implement some sort of grouping — things around the same timestamp could group, to make reading easier

or maybe i could do some fancy pants integration with my gcal, mark who i was chatting with when i wrote the note

thoughts? has anyone done anything similar?

text:
This board game is really good. Highly recommend

It's barely even a board game. It's a mystery novel with a few board game-like elements that you read and then try to solve.

Best with 2 or 3 people, but I think it'd work solo or with more as long as you're with a more cooperative group

The mysteries are well done and take like 2 or 3 hours to solve. They're not replayable, but they're so good it doesn't matter. You get 10 cases in the box, so it's a lot of content.

Best Sherlock simulator I've ever seen. Pick it up and play with a friend!

text:
Wild blueberries are indeed wild. 

"Wild blueberries grow in their natural ecosystem with no artificial breeding or genetic modification. The soil is never tilled. Growers nurture the plants by managing pests and removing obstacles to growth like weeds and rocks. They aren't planted by farmers—they spread through horizontal underground stem and root systems called rhizomes and cover the landscape like a carpet. 

It takes two years to grow one wild blueberry, and in commercial quantities they only grow in five places in the world: Maine, Quebec, Prince Edward Island, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick. The plants grow only about 12 inches tall (called "lowbush" to distinguish from cultivated "highbush" varieties that can reach 6 feet)."

text:
the soil there is tropical, and no glaciers passed over it recently, so it's basically toxic to plants. highly acidic, tons of aluminum

the solution was to just go crazy with lime. a massive one time application would reset the soil pH and make it plant friendly

raw.full_text:
the soil there is tropical, and no glaciers passed over it recently, so it's basically toxic to plants. highly acidic, tons of aluminum

the solution was to just go crazy with lime. a massive one time application would reset the soil pH and make it plant friendly

text: then, the plan was to grow soybeans. since brazil is equatorial, soybeans would grow way too fast, since they use day length to determine flowering. through selective breeding the ag researchers created a brazil appropriate soybean that would grow to full potential before producing beans

raw.full_text: then, the plan was to grow soybeans. since brazil is equatorial, soybeans would grow way too fast, since they use day length to determine flowering. through selective breeding the ag researchers created a brazil appropriate soybean that would grow to full potential before producing beans

text:
so the soybeans could grow, but they still needed to be fertilized. apparently soybeans get some of their nitrogen needs from symbiotic bacteria. Johanna Döbereiner spent her career finding the right bacteria that work in the treated Cerrado soil.

this was such a great success that now most brazilian soybeans get 100% of their nitrogen from bacteria — apparently the low nitrogen content of the soil pushes the plant to self-generate more. no nitrogen fertilizers required.

text:
for the state of Brandenburg-Prussia was fighting for its survival, yet even here Frederick's enemies were ready to 'pull their punches' to a surprising degree, doing nothing to close off Frederick's access to Poland, with its resources of grain, remounts, cattle and recruits. In fact the Emperor Francis Stephen was known to have made a thriving personal business of sending supplies to the Prussian army, his public opponent. In 1748 the same gentleman required the British fleet to blockade Genoa, while simul- taneously lodging a protest against the British action in his capacity as Grand Duke of Tuscany. This was an age of aristocratic values, another influence which militated against the vulgar passion of national hatred. French culture and the French language reigned supreme in what Gibbon called 'The Republic of Europe', and the cosmopolitan nobleman of the day cultivated a code of chivalry which emphasised that he owned more in common with the aristocrat of other states than with his own countrymen of common birth

- The Army of Frederick the Great, Christopher Duffy

text: The medium is the message applies to you too. Choosing what form your work takes sends a signal — Substack posts vs a single domain page imply different things. Look at AI essays like Aschenbrenner’s Situational Awareness. Putting it at a single URL makes it durable and shareable in a way a Substack post isn’t. A Substack post implies it’s part of a continuous series, a single element in a flow. Publishing a print-on-demand version of your blog posts says your body of work is worth taking seriously as a whole — an entirely new level of durability.

text: I’m not into basketball, but I thought it was interesting why the Knicks were in such rough shape for the last 20 years. The core issue was that even if the Knicks performed poorly, MSG still sold out and the team still made money. The owner had no incentive to actually ensure the team won - hence no real accountability.

text:
I test drove Hermes for a few weeks and came away... vaguely unimpressed. It's basically Claude Code with  memory and cron jobs.

I found it useful, but I kept asking myself — why am I using this and not Claude Code? Mostly it was the interface (I used Telegram). That, plus the Hetzner hosting costs, plus the API costs led me to shut it down recently. 

I think part of the reason may be because I didn't give it access to my email and such — I gave it a burner email and had it do non-personal tasks. It seems like the people who really enjoy using it are giving it full access to their life.

use cases
- russian writing practice
- finding old movies in theaters
- sending me the pressure fronts of the northeast from NOAA every day
- summarizing claude code and cursor changelogs 
- a few other things that didn't stick

I may give it another go — hosting it on my home server & using my Claude max sub for it rather than API key. it was nice to have around but I wouldn't pay extra for it.

text:
So generating tokens for a big LLM is slow, but verifying a set of tokens is fast. You can parallelize the token checking, which makes it much quicker.

You can make token generation faster by having a small model generate tokens, then verifying those tokens in the big model.

“It races ahead and guesses, say, the next 5 tokens. The big model then looks at all 5 in a single pass and goes "yep, yep, yep, nope" — it keeps everything up to the first wrong guess and corrects that one. Instead of 5 slow passes, you got maybe 3–4 good tokens out of one. That's your speedup.” — Claude’s explanation

What’s impressive is that the output will be the exact same. The tokens are all checked on the big model, so the output will be of the same quality. This is called speculative decoding and I thought it was super impressive!

Instead of 5 slow passes, you got maybe 3–4 good tokens out of one. That's your speedup.” — Claude’s explanation

What’s impressive is that the output will be the exact same. The tokens are all checked on the big model, so the output will be of the same quality. This is called speculative decoding and I thought it was super impressive!

text:
It’s interesting to me when seemingly de jure procedure outweighs de facto power. 

Mussolini got removed by King Victor Emmanuel III. Technically under the fascist regime - the constitution never got suspended. This meant that the king was still head of state. It didn’t mean much for a while, up until it became a channel through which elite resistance was coordinated. 

So the monarch didn’t have power, right up until it was convenient for him to. The belief that he did have power was self- fulfilling
